nodes.title: Use `filter` to keep only wanted values
nodes.why: `filter` naturally removes items that fail the check, which matches this problem.
nodes.alternative: Loop manually and push values into a new array.
nodes.tradeoff: Very concise and readable, but hides a bit of logic inside a helper call.

nodes.title: Pass `Boolean` as the predicate
nodes.why: `Boolean(value)` converts each item to true/false, so falsy values are dropped.
nodes.tradeoff: Short and idiomatic, though beginners may not recognize this pattern immediately.

nodes.title: Return filtered result directly
nodes.why: No intermediate variable is needed because `filter` already returns a new array.
nodes.alternative: Store the result in a variable before returning.
nodes.tradeoff: Compact and clean, but offers less room for step-by-step debugging.

nodes.title: Create a new output array
nodes.why: This keeps the input untouched and collects only values we want to keep.
nodes.alternative: Mutate the original array in place.
nodes.tradeoff: Safer behavior, with extra memory for the new array.

nodes.title: Push only truthy values
nodes.alternative: Write explicit comparisons for each falsy value.
nodes.tradeoff: Simple rule and easy to read, but less explicit about which values are removed.

nodes.title: Return the constructed array
nodes.why: After one pass through the input, `result` holds the compacted output.
nodes.tradeoff: Explicit flow is beginner-friendly, though longer than the one-liner version.
